CURRENT AFFAIRS.

There is another opening for a Post Office Dump into
TIreland from the predominant job filler. Castlerea is
in want of a Postmaster. The salary attached to the
position is £180 a year, together with a remarkable
allowance of £76 in respect of house rent. Even in
Dublin of the exorbitant house rents, one could get a
very good house for £76 a year, but in Castlerea! We
wonder is there any Castle vacant there? What chance
is there under the ‘ Liberal ’’ Government of an Irish-
man being a-ppoi.n‘tedb to this vacancy! As far as we
know, Mr. William Field, M.P., the universal sym-
patthiser, has not pursued his enquiry in respect to the
recent Birr Dump. What is the use of asking a ques-
tian if the matter is not pursued to the end, and if the®

possible? 'We hope the policy of “ Don’t embarrass the
Government ’* does not hold the field. A “ Liberal*®
Government waving “ Irish Ideas’ at a green people
ought to be embarrassed as much as possible for dump-
ing British Post Office officials into this country over
the heads of deserving and long-suffering Irish postal
officials.

Some Bavarian printers evidently have a mind te
change the geography of Ireland. We have 2 picture
post card, marked “ printed in Bavaria,” which pur
ports to represent  Howth, Co. Wicklow.”

In another column Mr. Michael Sullivan writes am
interesting article concerning the Parliamentary Re-
turn issued on the motion of Mr. MacVeagh. Our
readers who know Mr. Sullivan very well by repute, if
they do not, like Brother Goulding, know him per-
sonally, will be glad to hear that he is in splendid fozne
since—perhaps as a result of—his recent holidays im
Rome. Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan had the honour of am
audience with His Holiness the Pope. By the way,
“M. O'R.,” whose health we are glad to hear is im-
proved, had Mr. Sullivan as one of his guests at dinner
during the latter’s visit to the Eternal City.

The Parliamentary Return, amongst other things,
sets out the ages, or perhaps it would be more correck
to say the alleged ages, of the various persons specified.
Our personal acquaintance with officials of the = mne-
farious ”’ Government is very limited, yet we &now =
sufficient, number, personally or by sight, te enzble ms
to indulge in @ smile or two at the ages—or alleged
ages—of some of them!

Tommy Atkins’ bands will have plenty to do during
the progress of Humphrey’s Dump. We learn from a
paper called “ Besson’s Brass Band Budget,”” that the
band of the “ Irish Guards’’ will open the Dump, and
the Dublin correspondent of our contemporary addsz—
¢ . “and I rather think the opening ceremony will
be attended with a penformance by the massed Bands
of the Garrison with the Irish Guards, under My
Hassell, who, by the way, is no stranger in Dublin, e&
he will probably know how to make himself at home
here.”” Surely Brother Goulding ought to be present,
and should give a selection on the tamborine!

We gather from the same source that it is reporfed
that the band of the 2nd Royal Irish Fusiliers is bocked
for a seven weeks’ engagement at the Dump, and that
“Mr. Barwood, of the Royal Berks.,” has also secured
a four weeks’ engagement, and the Dublin Fusilier
Militia band one week.

By the way, what steps, if any, are being taken with
a view to protecting Dublin from the hordes of pick-
pockets, thieves, housebreakers, and immoral characters
that are likely to visit the city when Humphrey’s Dumg _

opens

There is every reason to hope that the moeting which,
as a 1*§sult of the imitiative of the Edenderry Board of
Guardians, met at the Mansion House, Dublin, one day
last, week, will have practical results. Tho standard-
ising of samples is a piece of practical work of great
importance to the industrial interests of Ireland. On
the motion of Mr. George Perry, the following resolu-
tion was adopted :— That a committee be formed from
the various Boards, consisting of four or more represen-
tatives from each Province in Ireland, to sclect and
adopt a set of Irish standard samples, of a size and
quality fit for general use in the various asylums and
other public imstitutions in Ireland. Sealed patterus
to be seen in each of the principal cities in.cach Pro-
vince, and tender forms to have particulars of where
patterns can be seen by intending contractors.’” Mr.
McKnight said that “The Industrial Development
Association didn’t expect that anyone would give more
for Irish goods than market prices. If the conference
succeeded in carrying out this standardising of samples
scheme, they would be able to manufacture om a larger
scale, and would be able to produce more cheaply.
Then in slack times they could put up a stock for future
requirements. Irish manufacturers didn’t ask to be
subsidised, all they asked for was support and for a
fair field.””

Accounts, and speeches about them, are very interest-
ing or very boring—it all depends on one’s relations to
them. Every solvent person is interested in his own
accounts—insolvent people would like to hide from
them ; but it would be a penance to be asked to spend
a few hours checking and examining figures in a strange
ledger in which one had no interest. ~ The Emglish
Budget has only a melancholy interest for Ireland.
Ireland knows that its money is in “the Treasury,” but
it knows that the needs of Ireland do not rufile the
spirit of the men at the till. A miserable few pounds
were taken from this country that should have been
spent on fees for Irish teaching in the Primary Schools,
but the Chancellor of the Exchequer has a surplus of
between three and four millions sterling!

The General Council of County Councils, on the
motion of Mr. Dolan, requested the Irish Parliamentary
Party to exert themselves with a view to having the
provisions of the Sunday Closing Act applied to St.
Patrick’s Day. The original demand was that the
public houses should be closed entirely on St. Patrick’s
Day. However, it is plain that unless an Act of Par-
liament is passed, the movement for closing the public
houses wholly or partially on that Day will fail.

Amongst the other resolutions passed was one asking
the National Board to recommend, and asking the Chief
Secretary and the Treasury to adopt, the scale of fees
put forward by the Gaelic League for the teaching of
Irish in National schools, and urging on the Chief
Secretary and the Treasury the claim of the Irish people
to have this provision for the teaching of the Irish lan-
guage instituted as soon as possible.

Mr. Sweetman’s name is not mentioned amongst the
list of those present. The resolution with reference to
the Parliamentary Party and the public houses on St.
Patrick’s Day would have proved awkward for him had
he been present. Besides the following resolution was
proposed and passed :— That we desire to convey %o
his Holiness Pius X., Supreme Head of the Catholic
Church, and to the Venerable Cardinal Anchbishop of

Paris represonting the Catholic clergy and laity of
France, the feelings of sorrow and surprise with which
we view recent acts of forcible intgr.ierence by the
Govenment of a professedly Catholic and Christian
nation with the worship of God, the authority of. the
Toly See, and the liberties of religious Communities,
sacred rights which are respected and safeguarded even
by non-Christian Powers.”” Had Mr. Sweetman been
present, he would not, we feel sure, hﬂ.ve oppoced this |
resolution; but if he acquiesced he might have been
charged with being one of the great “ plot ™ to organise
a now “ Brass Band ’’ which the organ of the party of
which he is a Vice-President has discovered !

Mr. D. J. O’Donoghue, in his Gcogl:aphﬁcal Dis-
tribution of Irish Ability,” writes:—“It is d.‘lﬂicg‘]b to
say why Leitrim should be the lowest of all counties in
intellectual achievement, but such it is.”’ In view of
this remark on Leitrim, it is significant that Leitrim is
the only county of all the thirty-two counties in Ire-
land that contributed nothing to the Language Fund
last year. Some other counties did not do very much
better tham nothing. The county of Kilkc)m‘y, which
includes the city of that name, and several fairly large
towns, only subscribed 16s. 5d., and that total sub-
seription of 16s. 5d. came from Sgoit Deapnan na
Gaoite. The previous year the county of Kilkenny
sent up £19 16s. 6d.; Fermanagh, last year, only sent
£3 0s. 0d.; Monagham only £4 8s. 2d.; the previous
year Monaghan subscribed £31 0s. 0d. The county of
Clavan, last year, only sent £8 2s. 0d.; Queen’s County
only £3 12s. 6d.; Carlow only £4 Os. 0d. The pre-
vious year Carlow county sent £13 3s. 6d.; Wicklow
county sent last year only £4 Os. 0d., against the pre-
vious year’s £11 14s. 0d.; Wexford sent £10 5s. 6d.
last year, as against £59 17s. 8d. the year previous.
Some of these represent rather serious drops, and,
judging by the last communicated report from the
Exccutive of the League, this year’s collection is an
anxious problem.

Of the thirty-one counties that contributed last year
—excluding Dublin, which contributed £543 4s. 11d.,
as against £510 11s. 1d. the previous year—Cork stands
first with a total of upwards of £161. Limerick comes
next with £132 10s. 0d.; then Antrim, which includes
Belfast, from whidh £116 0s. 0d. was received, comes °
with £122 15s. 3d. County Clare sent over £106,
Kerry over £88 0s. 0d., Tipperary over £61 0s. 0d.
County Waterford omly sent £52 1s. 1d., as against -
£104 19s. 6d. the previous year. Connacht, as a
province, sent very little—only £124 5s. 6d.—which is
considerably lees than the single county of Cork, less
than the county of Limerick, and not very much more
than the county of Clare. Munster contributed over
£600, Ulster £250, and Leinster, less Dublin which
subscribed £543, contributed £180. Last year the
Axgentine Republic sent more money to the Language
Fund than did the whole province of Connacht.

In so far as “money speaks,” there have been some
serious drops. How the collection has gone this year
we cannot say—the collection in Dublin will not take
place until June.

dance at last weel’s Spring Show as compared with
previous years.  To this result the clashing of the
Curragh vaces meeting with the Ballsbridge fixture
probably largely contvibuted. We heard our old friend

“ Humphrey’s Dump *’ referred to as one of the causes
of a reduced number of visitors, and, indeed, it isnot
unlikely that on that account a great many people who
would otherwise come up to the Spring Show, postponed
their visit to Dublin until a later season. There was
@ varied and interesting collection of general exhibits in
'th.e Central Hall and field, amongst which, as usual,
Irish manufactured products made a striking show.

. Evidently the demand for Inish manufactured feed-
img stuffs, etc., is encouraging, as we were glad to
notice that another fin—Germolas, Litd.,—has become
established in this branch of industry in Dublin since
last Spring Show. The extensivo space occupied by
Messrs. Edmondson Bros., seed merchants, Dame Streef,
contained a fine display of articles in their special line
—a prominent feature amongst them being an assortment
of Irish-made bee hives and appliances.  Messrs.
J. P. Evans and Co., the well known Limerick firm,
had an iuteresting display of various anticles in the
dairy machinery line at their stand in the Central Hall.
Messrs. Paul and Vincent, Dockrell and Son, Barmak,
Ltd., Wexford Engineering Co., Shannon Foundry,
Dairy Engincening Co., Dublin, were among many other
exhibitors that we also noticed.

Messrs. Pierce and Co., of Wexford, occupied the most
prominent, and, we think, the largest, position in the
big hall. This firm won the first prize and gold medal
at the Cork Annual Show, 1903-4, for best collection of
improved agricultural implements and modern machinery
in class open to the world; also the first prize and gold
medal in class restricted to Irish manufacturers. Messrs.
Pierce’s stand was a little National Exhibition in itself.
Here is a record of some of the firm’s victories in 1905
and 1906 :—France, at La Roche Sur Yonne, February,
1905, first prize and gold medal; Thurles, June, 1905,
first prize and gold medal; Athenry, June, 1905, first
prize and gold medal ; Enniscorthy, August, 1905, first
prize and gold medal; Shillelagh, August, 1905, first
prize and silver cup; Camolin, January, 1906, ﬁ!sb
prize and silver cup; Gorey, January, 1906, first prize
and silver cup; Tagoat (Wexford), February 1906,
first prize and special award; Ballaghkeene (Wexford),
February, 1906, first prize and special award; Bree
(Wexford), February, 1906, first prize and special
award. Messrs. Pierce also made a splendid display of
their famous ¢ Pierce’’ bicycles, and we can personally
testify to the worth of a Pierce bicycle,

The Irish Book Company have issued a cheap edition
of Canon O’Leary’s now famous book, Seaona. The
cheap edition, which is placed on the market ab 2s., is
bound in a stiff paper cover. The first edition of the
3s. 6d. book is, we hear, practically sold out, but a new
edition of that has also been printed.  Irish readers
have therefore a choice between the 2s. paper cover
edition, without portrait of the author, and the cloth-
bound edition, with portrait.

Graduates of the Royal University will be particu-
larly intcrested in the following from the English
“ Morning Post,”” for the prospective Deputy Grand
King irroverently called Jimmy > by the '\xxxdell'gljads.
is one of the paid secrotaries of the University witlh: the
“TLoyal > organ:— The installation of Sir James Creed
Meredith, LL.D., as Deputy Grand King of the Grand
Royal Arch Chapter of Ireland will take place in the
Masonic Hall, Dublin, on the evening of the Ist of
May. In the unavoidable absence of the Duke of Aber-
corn, K.G., the Grand King, the ceremony will be per-
formed by the M.E., Frank H. Wayland, H.P., the pre-
sent senior grand officer, who will be assisted by several
other actual and past grand officers. An unprecedently
large gathering of Irish Royal Arch Masons is expected
on the occasion, as Sir James Creed Meredith is already
Deputy Grand Master of Craft Masonry in Ireland.”
Deputy Ri Seumap ata! The undergrads. should have
a torchlight procession on the 1st of May!

We received half a dozen replies to the query from
an Irish lady residing in England with reference to a
pedigree Irish terrier pup. We have sent on the
names to our correspondent. One of those who replied
deserves special mention, Mr. P. F. Clery, Clonmel, as
he adventised.  Another sent us a sealed letter re-
questing us to address it and forward to our corres-
pondent, which request we, of course, complied with.
Another writing cn behalf of a friend, remarked,
amongst other things, that his friend “acts as judge in
most dog shows in Ireland and England, and ezhibits
himself.” The italics are ours. Our correspondent,
acknowledging a letter from us enclosing the names of
four of those who had replied at the time of writing,
says in the course of her note:—“I feel embarrassed
by the wideness of the choice now presented to me, and
am conscious of an unhappy sort of feeling that I ought
to buy puppies from each and all of the gentlemén
mentioned in your letter.”’ 5

per week during the session, of about 40 weeks.

THE IRISH MANUFACTURE QUESTION.

N his letter in your issue of Gth inst. on the indus-
I trial situation your correspondent, “Industrial
Revivalist,”” says absolutely nothing new on the sub-
ject, except that in his locality—the Midlands—the
people not only ignove, but they “make a laugh of Irish
manufactures.” ~ Well, all 1 can say is, if that be so
they are past praying for. But I'hope it is confined to a,
small locality, as I should be very sorry to think there
is any such treachery and devilment '’ (his words)
abroad in Ireland. Certainly I have not heard of such
in trade before. I know that there is apathy, stupid
and widespread apathy, among the people of this coun-
try with regard to supporting their own manufactures,
but I think that is the worst that can be said of them
as a people. I think this indifference is frequently
prompted by want of thought. Indeed, several causes
go to produce it. First, there is what, in legal phrase,
is called “ use and wont.”” People have been used for
years buying certain goods not made in Ireland, and,
from force of habit, go on buying them. The quality is
good, the price all right, and simply, it never occurs to
them to make any change. Again, the credit system
has a good deal to do with this indifference.

In every town and village in Ireland customers are
indebted to their shopkeeper 10s. or 20s., or perhaps
two or three or five pounds, or more. This being so,
they cannot insist on being supplied with Irish goods.
Again, very many shopkeepers are just in the same state
of indebtedness to the wholesale merchant, with a simi-
lar result. In their turn the merchant is very often
indebted to the manufacturer, who frequently happens
to be English or Scotch, or what is worse, “pro-Bri-
tish,’” and all this tells against home manufacture.’

Another cause is the Irish agent for foreign goods.
He spends his time and energy in inducing 'his Irish
friends to buy foreign ware, and so helps to keep our
working classes idle in their own country. Then there
is the proverbial difficulty of dislodging the man in pos-
session; and in this case the foreigner is the man in
possession—holding off a few things—ships, shirts, linen,
intoxicants, etc. Now these causes that I have stated
are chief amongst those that operate against Irish manu-
facture.

What is the remedy? Oh, a dozen remedies are
offered. The Gaelic League, Sinn Fein, the A.Q.H.,
the Irvish Foresters, and many other socicties, all put
Irishe manufacture as a main plank in their platforms.
Join us, they say, and the people will be employed, and
all will be well; Ireland will flourish. Moreover, we
have the very expensive D.A.T.I. pledged to the fur-
therance of the good cause; and, at the back of all, a
patriotic and powerful press (Tam LEaper in this being
truly a loader) exploiting the subject every day and
every week. What is the upshot?

The upshot is that 4,668 persons emigrated from Ire-
land last year in excess of the number that emigrated in
1905. And this increase is being maintained down to
yesterday—the excess emigration of J. anuary, February
and March, 1907, being well accentuated over the cor-
responding quarter of 1906. Ask any stationmaster in
Ireland, or any steamboat agent at any of our ports,
as %o the proportion of imported goods their companies
lcwr:iy, and the answer will be surprising to an Irish Ire-
ancder.

Indeed, _the least observant traveller must see at
every station, wharf, warehouse, or village shop, the
vast number of packages, full and empty, of all sorts

probable development of manufacturing industry would _
necessarily stop the exodus of raw human material from

IRISH DANCING.

HE Maynooth Decrees had arrived a short time
before when a friend and myself came across a
couple of priests, who, like ourselves, are interested in
Irish affairs, and cspecially in the Gaolic League. The
faces of these men wore a puzzled, anxious look. Each
had a copy of the Decrees in his hand. As we
approached, without waiting for the usual salutations
in Irish, one of them informed us in plain English that
oné item of the Gaelic League programme, and one
which we considered very important—for we had often
discussed the question—had been not indeed condermed
by their Lordships, but had been very seriously dis-
couraged. The ruling of the Bishops is to this effect—
I took a note of it. The clergy are to avoid altogether,
they are not to institute or promote dances either public
or private. We, too, knew Latin, at least a little, and
we discussed for some time what might be the mdaning
of “choreas,”” which the clergy are to avoid, not to
institute or promote same as “saltationes’’ dances in
the ordinary sense. Further, our attention was called
to the fact that the clergy were to oppose witlh all their
power dances repugnant to Christian modesty, and that
they were not to permit dancing in any house where there
would be an immoderate use of drink, or where, in the
absence of elderly people, the young of both sexes come
together. We all welcomed these latter restrictions,
but we were not a little puzzled to know whether there
might not be some explanation of the former, which is
an equivalent condemnation of all dancing.

My friend and I, both of us Gaelic Leaguers, and
very much interested in everything which is calculated
to improve the social condition of our Irish people at
home, took our departure, wondering very much what
could be the meaning of it; did their Lordships really
discountenance, which would be tantamount to a con-
demnation of, all dancing? Or was it that only certain
classes of dancing were meant—viz., those that are
opposed to Christian modesty? We could not, of
course, settle the point.

Later on, the same evening, we went to see a little
Irish play. The first scene was laid in a country house
in Donegal. It wasa Céitrde. The piper was theve;
and wihen he struck up a tune three couples stood forth
for a reel. Reels and jigs were gone through, and an
occasional song sung. While the dancing was in pro-
gress my friend remarked—May it be that the Bishops
did not understand, not having an opportunity of know-
ing exactly, what Irish dancing really is. I replied
that I did not know, but that it was quite possible, con-
sidering the state of society in this country, till the
Gaelic League began to make itself felt, that Irish danc-
ing may have been confounded with those other impor-
tations which we all so heartily detest. Since we first
saw dancing we never could observe anything in Irish
dances which could excite a suspicion of the danger of
them to morality. And if observation affords not the

ing, to & middle-aged lady. “ When I was at home,”
she sa_.id, “T danced rings round me (an expression; com-
mon in some places, for a great deal), and I don’t think
I ever committed a sin in dancing.’” My friend and I
parted thiat night with the 'hope that dancing of the
night kind, dancing which is real dancing, not pulling
and trailing, in a word Irish dancing, a very potent
factor in Ireland’s social life, was not meant to be dis-
couraged, and that it would be taken up and super-
vised, to whatever extent supervision is necessary, by
those who have charge of the spiritual welfare of the
Irish people.
I do not at all question the authority of the Bishops
to interfere in matters of this kind where they may
deem it necessary. I am a Catholic, and proud of my
religion. I stand stoutly by the spiritual aubhority of
those placed over me, and yield to none in respect for
and submission to the will of the Irish Hierarchy.
Nevertheless, I cannot help thinking, though I submit
I may be wrong in thinking, that a very great mistake
will be made if the Bishops mean to discourage Irish
dancing, and persist in that attitude towards a form of
pastime so dear to the people. We have all been
deploring, and do deplore, the stream of emigration
which is wasting and carrying off the ““ bone and sinew ”’
of Irish manhood and Irish womanhood. We are, as
we have been for years, crying down emigration and
emigration agency, direct and indirect, by which Ire-
land at home is being reduced to the condition of a
Bedlam and nursery combined. The Gaelic League set
itself to stem the stream, but the stream still flows and
flows with increasing rapidity and increasing volume.
The means employed by the Gaelic League, approved by
the Bishops, as by everybody who has the interests of
this country at heart, were, and still are, the revival
of Irish industry and the revival of social life.  The
aim of the national movement is to make it at least pos-
sible for the people to remain at home. That implies
first of all that they have the means of supporting them-
selves, but scarcely less does it imply that they must
have opportunities occasionally for mind and heart and
limb to get away from the ordinary environment, and
be relaxed for a time from the worries of life. It im-
plies, in a word, that they are not to be condemned to
unending drudgery about the house or in the field, and
tied down to eternal moping in what is practically soli-
tude. And the dance is the pastime the Irish people
naturally turn to, and is in fact almost the only one
possible for the great majority of them. From time
immemorial the dance in the kitchen, at the cross-roads,
and in the fair was the main pastime and chief factor in
the social life of our people. And I don’t know that
they were anything the worse for it. But I do know
that the Irish were, and are to-day, the most chaste
people on this planet of ours. What, then, has taken
place of late years that dancing should be suspected by
the Irish Bishops? A change had taken, or was taking,
place when their Lordships met in council in Maynooth
in 1900. Foreign dancing and foreign music were find-
ing their way into the lives of the people of this country,
and ousting out the music and dance that are native
to the soil. But the last seven years have witnessed a
change in Irish life. ~ Wihat in 1900 may have been
regarded as evil or dangerous, has in 1907 all but dis-
appeared. And under the guidance of their I.o.rdships
and the clergy, within the next few years we might see
our native pastimes restored to the place from which
they should never have been allowed to depart.

N eminent dignitary of ours, whose pronounce-

friends might use it to shield their retreat, and open
enomies might twist it to a grappling _‘hqu wherewith
to swamp us. Far be it from me to criticise his utter-
ances; but I would lift my voice for those who might
be exposed to danger. His words, I believe, were as
follows : — "

“T never blame & man for passing over an article of
TIrish manufacture, if it is not equal to that which can
be had from any other country.””

Then he mentioned two instances in whidh he had
been disappointed with Irish decorative work. One
was a case of dishonest work, and to suchi I do not
refer. The obher was a case of lhonest work unskilfully
done, a matter of wood carving on church benches. The
result, he said, ““ was a show.”

With all respect, I beg leave to submit that the
blame lice at the door of the person who gave the con-
tract to this carver, without first taking steps to ascer-
tain whether hie was capable of carrying out the design.
If he had found that this workman was not equal to
the task, he might, with little trouble, have found
other Irish carvers who could carry out with a fair
amount of artistic skill whatever design was set them.
Or—and this suggestion I would emphasise—if, for any
reason, it was desirable to employ Uhis carver, the
design should have been modified to the measure of his
skill. Give a craftsman work he can do, and then take
care that he puts his heart into it. If he does, he will
soon do wall ; but ’tis a bad gardener neglects his tree if
the early fruit is bitter. Spoil your gardener, spoil
your garden. Yet I fear our eminent dignitary’s
charity inclines on the side of leniency towards his
gardener.

Irish art craftsmen may not, as a body, be all that
we could wish. It rests with us—as buyers and
employers—to make them better. ~And we must
remember art is a plant of slow growth, and needs
patient nurture, so we will not expect to find a race
of artists spring into being at once, wherever, Dzdalus-
like, we pile our stones. Personally, I should decidedly
blame an Irishman for passing over native work, if it
is not yet always equal to that which can be had from
some other country, provided it is sufficiently good,
and assuming that it is the result of honest hard work.
Give our craftsmen more to do, and they will soon give
us better value. Meanwhile, there is something better
for us to do with life than to spend it calculating
whether we get exactly two hundred and fifty-two times
a pennyworth of pre-Raphaelitism for every guinea we
give poor Paddy Murphy. Is the odd penny badly in-
vested if it keeps Paddy in Ireland, and helps him to
become skilled in his craft, and to rear more craftsmeu
who will be yet more skilful? Perhaps his grandson
will be our Michael Angelo.

It is not racial vanity that makes me believe the
national temperament contains the elements from which
artists spring. Greater authorities admit it. And we
have, thank God, what is more—the intuition begotten
of true faith which 'has ever been the inspiration of
those who have given earth here rarvest treasures.
“ Why, then,” sneers somebody, “ have we so little art
alive to-day?’ “How can we expect,”” whines some-
body else, “to find art, which is a luxury, abounding
in this down-trodden and poverty-stricken country?’’
It is not because of our poverty. Are we not rich enough

RE heroes an extinet class? I do not mean the
mythical hero, whose cloudy home is the Abbey
Theatre, nor yet the amateur hero, the life-saver, ctc.,
of our own time. Though enjoying no personal ac-
quaintance with the former, I have known some of the
latter and they were quite ordinary, even humane,
people in private life. I am thinking rather of your
professional hero, your protagomist of romance, who
draws a large income out of six-shilling fiction in
youth, to spend it (I am sure) in niotous living, and eke
out a miserable old age in sixpenny reprints. Poor
heroes, I believe you an extinct class. The most unfit,
you have inevitably failed to survive in modern strife.

If a young man never did what was right even by
accident, lost all his money at play, persisted in falling
in love with his nearest friend’s adored one, consistently
disobeyed his superior’s ordeis, and in fact made a
prodigious ass of himself cn the smallest available oppor-
tunity, could such a one hope to survive in the twen-
tieth century? I am sure your hero would be hopelessly
plucked in the Preparatory Grade, he never could pass
even the Trinity entrance; I doubt if he would succeed in
getting into his favourite profession, the army, even
through the medium of the militia. We have no room
for heroes. If they exist at all, I suspect they are to
be found filling those public positions that are not ob-
tainable by open competition.

But, gentle reader, you are saying that let society
think what it may, the dearest girl in the world is
always ready to take your hero to her bosom (illustra-

Christian than other heroines, being almost entirely

selves priestly vows must—or ought to—be sure that
they are marked out for some express service. What
might be an act of splendid obedience, sublime self-
renunciation in a Newman, would be presumption and
folly in a lesser spirit. I hope you agree.”

May one express a deeply reverent sense of the philo-
sophical learning of Mrs. Craigie? Still she claims such
familiarity with Hegel that one is rather tempted to
doubt that her knowledge of Plato, Aristotle, Spinoza
and Newman can be due to first-hand acquaintance. For
most of us have to confess that we have tackled Hegel in
vain, and simply do not pretend to understand him.

J. C. M‘WALTER.
